coredata.dc:title: Improvement of the detection efficiency of 3M™ molecular detection system for Campylobacter in poultry using nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots
coredata.dc:creator: Ha, Jimyeong
Seo, Yeongeun
Cho, Eunbee
Choi, Seungho
Kim, Hojin
Yoon, Yohan
coredata.dc:description: This study was performed to examine whether the use of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) can improve the detection sensitivity of the 3 M™ molecular detection system (MDS) for Campylobacter. N-CNDs were added to a Campylobacter enrichment broth (CEB) at concentrations of 5 and 10 mg/mL (NCEB-5 and NCEB-10, respectively). Campylobacter coli, C. jejuni, and C. lari were inoculated into the broths. The broth cultures were then irradiated with light-emitting diode (LED) at 425 nm for 1 h and incubated at 42 °C for 6 h, and then grown on modified charcoal cefoperazone deoxycholate agar (mCCDA). The detection rates of the MDS and a conventional method (plating an enriched sample on mCCDA and analyzing a colony on mCCDA with PCR) for Campylobacter in chicken and duck carcasses were compared. The detection rates from the MDS were compared after enrichment in CEB and NCEB-5 at 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12, and 24 h. When 5 mg/mL of N-CNDs was added to the CEB followed by irradiation at 425 nm, growth of the Campylobacter was accelerated. In addition, the qualitative test was more sensitive in the MDS than in the conventional method, and the detection time was shortened in CEB enriched with N-CNDs. These results indicate that adding N-CNDs to CEB can improve the detection efficiency of MDS.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Campylobacter
Detection
Molecular detection system
Enrichment broth
Nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of N-CNDs concentration in the enrichment medium
Comparison between the detection efficiency of conventional method and MDS
Detection efficiencies of CEB and NCEB-5 in the MDS
Concentration of N-CNDs for Campylobacter detection
Comparison of detection efficiency between conventional method and MDS
Effect of N-CNDs on the detection efficiency of MDS

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Campylobacter spp. concentrations in Campylobacter enrichment broth (CEB) supplemented with 0-, 5- and 10-mg/mL nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) after 6-h enrichment (n = 4). (A): C. coli; (B): C. jejuni; (C): C. lari. The error bar means standard error.

PCR results for duck samples examined according to the method described in the Korea Food Code. Lane M: 100-bp ladder; lane 1: positive control; lane 2: duck sample number 20; lane 3: duck sample number 21; lane 4: duck sample number 24; lane 5: duck sample number 27; lane 6: duck sample number 28.
Oligonucleotide primers used in the study.
Positive detection rate (%) of Campylobacter in chicken and duck carcasses.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NCEB-5: CEB supplemented with nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots at 5 mg/mL.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The assessment of the detection efficiency of the MDS for duck carcasses 11–20 after enrichment in CEB (Campylobacter enrichment broth) and NCEB-5 (CEB supplemented with N-CNDs at 5 mg/mL) at 42 °C at different time points.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NCEB-5: CEB supplemented with N-CNDs at 5 mg/mL.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The assessment of the detection efficiency of the MDS for duck carcasses 21–30 after enrichment in CEB (Campylobacter enrichment broth) and NCEB (CEB supplemented with N-CNDs at 5 mg/mL) at 42 °C at different time points.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: NCEB-5: CEB supplemented with N-CNDs at 5 mg/mL.

head.ce:title: Improvement of the detection efficiency of 3M™ molecular detection system for Campylobacter in poultry using nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Harim Institute of Life Science, Harim Corporation, Iksan 54538, Republic of Korea

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: Department of Food and Nutrition, Sookmyung Women's University, Seoul 04310, Republic of Korea.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This study was performed to examine whether the use of nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) can improve the detection sensitivity of the 3 M™ molecular detection system (MDS) for Campylobacter. N-CNDs were added to a Campylobacter enrichment broth (CEB) at concentrations of 5 and 10 mg/mL (NCEB-5 and NCEB-10, respectively). Campylobacter coli, C. jejuni, and C. lari were inoculated into the broths. The broth cultures were then irradiated with light-emitting diode (LED) at 425 nm for 1 h and incubated at 42 °C for 6 h, and then grown on modified charcoal cefoperazone deoxycholate agar (mCCDA). The detection rates of the MDS and a conventional method (plating an enriched sample on mCCDA and analyzing a colony on mCCDA with PCR) for Campylobacter in chicken and duck carcasses were compared. The detection rates from the MDS were compared after enrichment in CEB and NCEB-5 at 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12, and 24 h. When 5 mg/mL of N-CNDs was added to the CEB followed by irradiation at 425 nm, growth of the Campylobacter was accelerated. In addition, the qualitative test was more sensitive in the MDS than in the conventional method, and the detection time was shortened in CEB enriched with N-CNDs. These results indicate that adding N-CNDs to CEB can improve the detection efficiency of MDS.

ce:keyword.ce:text: synthesized N-CNDs were added to Campylobacter enrichment broth at concentrations of 5 mg/mL

ce:section.ce:para: Campylobacter is an infectious bacterium that causes illness in both humans and animals, and is commonly found in the gastrointestinal tracts of dogs, sheep, and poultry (USDA, 2013). Campylobacter infection can also be caused by the consumption of contaminated raw or undercooked poultry (Hodges et al., 2019). Campylobacter coli, C. jejuni, and C. lari are the most important Campylobacter species in foodborne illnesses (Frasao et al., 2017). They can cause serious diseases such as Guillain-Barre syndrome in humans, and thus, it is important to detect them quickly to prevent infection (Lee et al., 2019).

Campylobacter detection and isolation are difficult because they require specific conditions for growth, such as low oxygen concentration (Frasao et al., 2017). Because Campylobacter is weak against competition against other bacteria, it is important to control the growth of other bacteria during the enrichment process to enhance its detection (Chon et al., 2011). Most Campylobacter selective media contain antibiotics to inhibit the growth of competitive bacteria during enrichment, but antibiotic-resistant bacteria can proliferate in such media (Jo et al., 2017; Kim et al., 2020; Rao, 1998). This makes Campylobacter detection difficult. Thus, Ha et al. (2021) developed nitrogen-doped carbon nanodots (N-CNDs) to improve selectivity for detecting Campylobacter during enrichment, and developed a monoclonal antibody to detect Campylobacter after enrichment (Ha et al., 2021). The N-CNDs in the enrichment medium adhere to the bacteria, and they generate heat when exposed to 425-nm LED. This exothermic action destroys the bacterial cells. However, Campylobacter cells are hardly destroyed because the N-CNDs are hardly attached to Campylobacter cells due to their small cell size and spiral shape (Ha et al., 2021).

Various molecular detection methods for Campylobacter have been developed. Among them, the polymerase chain reaction (PCR) method is widely used because it is fast and simple (Raja et al., 2017; Sails et al., 2003). Recently, a loop-mediated isothermal amplification (LAMP) assay, that can be visualized with the naked eye, was developed (Babu et al., 2020). The LAMP assay is a method for amplifying DNA at a constant temperature, with an amplification efficiency approximately 1000 times higher than that of PCR (Kursa et al., 2015). In addition, it is easy to apply in the field as it does not require equipment such as a thermal cycler (Parida et al., 2008). Accordingly, a molecular detection system (MDS) for Campylobacter detection was developed by 3M™. The detection method of this system uses the LAMP principle; however, 24 h of enrichment is essential before successful detection. Because the qualitative results of Campylobacter detection can be obtained within 1 h using the MDS, remarkably rapid detection is possible when a method that shortens the enrichment time is developed.
Therefore, the objective of this study was to examine whether the use of N-CNDs can improve the detection sensitivity of the MDS for Campylobacter in poultry meat.

Campylobacter coli ATCC33559 (American Type Culture Collection, Virginia, USA), C. jejuni ATCC33560, and C. lari ATCC35223 were stored at −70 °C in a bead stock (AES Chemunex, Combourg, France). One of the beads was streaked on Columbia agar (BioMérieux, Marcy-l'Étoile, France) and incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions (CO2: 2.5%–9.5%, O2: 6.2%–13.2%) using Campygen (CampyGen gas generating system, Oxoid Ltd., Basingstoke, UK). Colonies were collected using a loop and were re-streaked onto Columbia agar, and the plates were incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions. The colonies were collected in 5 mL of phosphate-buffered saline (PBS; 0.2 g KH2PO4, 1.5 g Na2HPO4·7H2O, 8.0 g NaCl, and 0.2 g KCl in 1 L of distilled water; pH 7.4). The bacterial suspensions were centrifuged at 1,912 ×g at 4 °C for 15 min and washed twice with PBS. The cell pellets were resuspended in PBS. Eventually, serial 10-fold dilutions were prepared to achieve 2 log CFU/mL.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of N-CNDs concentration in the enrichment medium
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the appropriate concentration, synthesized N-CNDs were added to Campylobacter enrichment broth (CEB; 3 M, Saint Paul, MN, USA) at concentrations of 5 mg/mL (NCEB-5) and 10 mg/mL (NCEB-10), as previously described (Ha et al., 2021). Five hundred microliter of C. coli, C. jejuni, and C. lari inocula (4 log CFU/mL for each bacterium) were inoculated into 5 mL each broth (n = 4). The broth culture was then irradiated with 425 nm LED for 1 h in a dark box and enriched at 42 °C for 6 h. The cultures were plated on modified charcoal cefoperazone deoxycholate agar (mCCDA; Oxoid Ltd., Basingstoke, UK) and incubated at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions. Colonies were manually counted.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison between the detection efficiency of conventional method and MDS
ce:section.ce:para: Chicken (n = 10) or duck (n = 30) carcasses were purchased from Seoul. They were aseptically placed in zipper bags containing 400 or 800 mL of 0.1% buffered peptone water (BPW; Becton, Dickinson and Company, Sparks, MD, USA) for the chicken and duck carcasses, respectively. The samples were manually shaken for 1 min.

For the conventional method, 30 mL of the rinsates and 30 mL of 2× Bolton broth (Oxoid Ltd., Basingstoke, UK) supplemented with cefoperazone, trimethoprim, vancomycin, and cycloheximide were mixed and enriched at 42 °C for 48 h under microaerobic conditions according to the Korea Food Code (MFDS, 2020). The enriched samples were streaked on mCCDA, and the plates were incubated at 42 °C under microaerobic conditions for 48 h. The typical colonies on the plates were collected in microcentrifuge tubes and suspended in 0.2 mL of sterile distilled water, followed by boiling at 100 °C for 10 min and centrifugation at 15,602 ×g for 3 min at 4 °C; the supernatants were used as DNA templates. Sterile distilled water (14 μL), DNA (2 μL), and primers (2 μL) (Table 1 ) (Denis et al., 1999) were mixed in the FAST mix frenche kit (iNtRON Biotechnology, Inc., Seongnam, Korea). The amplification profile was: initial denaturation at 95 °C for 5 min, followed by 35 cycles of 95 °C for 30 s, 58 °C for 90 s, and 72 °C for 60 s. The PCR product was then electrophoresed on a 2% agarose gel in 1× TAE buffer (Biosesang, Gyeonggi-do, Korea) at 110 V for 40 min. The size of the product was confirmed by visualizing the bands on a UV-transilluminator (WUV-M20, DAIHAN Scientific, Wonju-si, Korea).

To evaluate the detection efficiency of the MDS, the rinsates (25 mL) and CEB (25 mL) were mixed and enriched at 42 °C for 24 h under aerobic conditions. In addition, to enrich the rinsates in NCEB-5, 25 mL of the rinsates were centrifuged at 1912 ×g for 15 min at 4 °C, and the supernatant was discarded. Thereafter, the pellets were suspended in 2.5 mL BPW. The suspension (2.5 mL) and NCEB-5 (2.5 mL) were mixed, followed by irradiation with 425 nm LED for 1 h. They were then enriched at 42 °C for 24 h under aerobic conditions. After enrichment, the Campylobacter isolates were detected using the 3 M™ MDS and the 3 M™ Molecular Detection Assay 2 – Campylobacter MDA2CAM96 in accordance with the manufacturer's protocols. The MDS used isothermal amplification technology and bioluminescence detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection efficiencies of CEB and NCEB-5 in the MDS
ce:section.ce:para: A total of 10 chicken and 20 duck carcasses were aseptically transferred to zipper bags containing 400 or 800 mL 0.1% BPW, respectively. The samples were manually shaken for 1 min. The rinsates were enriched in CEB and NCEB-5 as described above, and the enriched samples were analyzed in the MDS at 3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 12, and 24 h after enrichment.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Concentration of N-CNDs for Campylobacter detection
ce:section.ce:para: When N-CNDs were added to CEB at 5 mg/mL (NCEB-5) and 10 mg/mL (NCEB-10), Campylobacter cell counts increased to 3 log CFU/mL or more within 6 h, which is the detection limit of the MDS. The increased cell counts of Campylobacter spp. after enrichment were higher with 5 mg/mL N-CNDs than with 10 mg/mL of N-CNDs (Fig. 1 ). In addition, there was no significant difference between the addition of N-CNDs and the non-addition of N-CNDs. This means that the addition of 5 mg/mL N-CNDs does not affect the growth of Campylobacter. In a previous study using Bolton broth, the addition of at least 5 mg/mL N-CNDs controlled the growth of other competing bacteria such as Acinetobacter baumannii or Escherichia coli (Ha et al., 2021). Taken together, 5 mg/mL was determined as the appropriate concentration to be included in CEB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Comparison of detection efficiency between conventional method and MDS
Effect of N-CNDs on the detection efficiency of MDS

ce:section.ce:para: In conclusion, when confirming the prevalence of Campylobacter in poultry, using MDS can reduce the time and increase the detection rate compared to conventional method. In addition, the detection efficiency of the MDS was improved by adding N-CNDs to CEB.
This research did not receive any specific grant from any specific funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

J. Microbiol. Methods
